title: storage-snapshot-cache: ship .jj/jjforge-cache.json
body:
# Goal

Implement the read-path snapshot cache pinned in
`docs/storage-index-design.md` (closing comment on `b9f628b`).
Measured wall-clock: `jjf ready` at N=1000 drops from 22.5s to
~15ms on a cache hit. At N=10000 the extrapolation is ~225s →
~25ms.

# Approach

Per the design doc:

- Single file at `.jj/jjforge-cache.json` (`.jj/` is already
  gitignored).
- Schema: `{ schema_version, head_commit, issues:
  BTreeMap<IssueId, Issue>, memories: BTreeMap<String, Memory>,
  slug_index: BTreeMap<String, IssueId> }`.
- On every read, probe the bookmark head via
  `jj log -r bookmarks(issues) -T commit_id --no-graph --limit 1`
  (~15ms).
- If `cache.head_commit == bookmark_head` → load and use the
  cache. Cache-hit cost: ~15ms regardless of N.
- Mismatch → rebuild via working-copy walk (`jj edit
  bookmarks(issues)` + `read_dir("issues/")` + `serde_json`,
  then `jj new root()` to step `@` off). Rebuild cost at
  N=10000: ~5s.
- Writers don't touch the cache. Every write moves the bookmark;
  the next read sees the mismatch and rebuilds.

# Surfaces

Rework these to consult the cache first:

- `Storage::list_ids`
- `Storage::list_ready`
- `Storage::list_memories`
- `Storage::resolve` (slug)
- `Storage::read` (single-issue read)
- `Storage::dep_tree`
- `Storage::find_open_slug_collision`

# Failure modes

- Cache file missing → rebuild.
- Cache file corrupt JSON → log info-level warning to stderr,
  rebuild.
- Schema version mismatch → rebuild.
- `.jj/` non-writable → fall back to in-memory cache for this
  process invocation, info-level warning to stderr.

# Constraints

- Pure cache. Never the source of truth. Rebuildable from scratch.
- No new runtime deps. We already have serde + serde_json.
- Tests: the existing 353-test workspace must stay green. Add a
  regression test that verifies a write through the storage layer
  invalidates the cache on the next read (`head_commit` mismatch
  → rebuild → fresh data visible).

# Out of scope

- A real index (rusqlite / redb). See the closing recommendation
  on `b9f628b`: cache is sufficient through 10k. If reads later
  prove slow even with the cache, the design doc has the
  fallback pinned.
- A `jjf cache` CLI verb. Operators don't need one; the cache is
  invisible.
- Persistence-format optimization (bincode / cbor). JSON parse at
  10k issues is ~10ms — not the hot path.

# Acceptance

- [ ] `.jj/jjforge-cache.json` is written on first read.
- [ ] Second read with no intervening write hits the cache
  (verifiable by stat: the bench from `experiments/scale-index/`
  reports the right number).
- [ ] A write followed by a read sees the new data (cache
  invalidates correctly).
- [ ] Workspace tests stay green.
- [ ] Benchmark numbers reproduce: cache-hit ~15ms at every N
  up to 10k.

# Pointers

- `docs/storage-index-design.md` — the full design.
- `experiments/scale-index/` — the benchmark.
- `crates/jjf-storage/src/lib.rs` — the surfaces to rework.
